network.overall_strategy: This network centers threonine synthesis on literature-supported cyanosulfidic Strecker chemistry, using HCN-derived aldehydes and aminonitriles as the main productive route, while adding empirically observed but less selective environmental sources from spark discharge and HCN polymer hydrolysis. The densest hubs are HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and the threonine aminonitrile. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks such as formate, acetaldehyde, and pyruvate under Fe-S mineral catalysis; surface photochemistry converts HCN into aldehydes and threonine precursors on UV-exposed mineral settings; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete hydrolysis and rapid utilization of unstable threonine into peptides. Speculative steps are explicitly labeled where the literature supports only principle-level plausibility rather than direct demonstration for threonine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent, H2-rich, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases generates C1 reduced carbon as formate.
reactions.reasoning: FeS/Fe3S4 minerals in alkaline vent systems are established catalysts for CO2 reduction and organic carbon fixation analogs; this step provides hydrothermal reduced carbon feeding surface homologation chemistry (White et al., 2015; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of acetaldehyde from reduced C1 feedstocks

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal, anoxic; aldehydes formed within broader FTT product mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Sequential reduction and C-C bond formation on iron oxide/metal surfaces generate small aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on magnetite/iron is widely invoked to generate aldehydes and hydrocarbons, while acetaldehyde is a key simple carbonyl present in abiotic product mixtures relevant to threonine pathways; used here conservatively as a feedstock source rather than direct threonine synthesis (Cody et al., 2000; Shock and Canovas, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: hydrothermal vent analog; reduced sulfur present

reactions.mechanism: Fe-S-promoted carbon fixation yields alpha-keto acids, here represented by pyruvate as a central hydrothermal keto-acid hub.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from carbonylated iron-sulfur systems under hydrothermal conditions is experimentally established and supplies a metabolically relevant hub for downstream amino acid chemistry (Cody et al., 2000).

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with Cu(I) cyanide chemistry supported in sulfide-rich, UV-active mineral setting
conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S as reductant, phosphate-buffered shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes photoredox homologation under UV irradiation, producing reduced carbonyl compounds through Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanide cycling and sulfide reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally demonstrated UV photoredox conversion of HCN into formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde using Cu(I) cyanide complexes and H2S. Patel et al. integrated this chemistry into cyanosulfidic protometabolism. Mineral agents are included as surface plausibility anchors required by the network schema, while Cu(I) cyanide is specified in conditions as the immediate photoredox system (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface aldol homologation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool or hot spring; wet-dry concentration possible

reactions.mechanism: Aldol homologation of lower aldehydes generates triose sugars, with borate stabilizing higher-order sugars against degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a critical branch point in Sutherland-type chemistry and is generated from simpler aldehydes under prebiotic sugar-forming conditions; borate minerals are specifically known to stabilize sugar products and thereby support this branch point connecting RNA and threonine aminonitrile synthesis (Patel et al., 2015; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Surface cyanohydrin formation from acetaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: HCN-rich, anoxic, shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetaldehyde gives the corresponding cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is an explicit intermediate in the Ritson-Sutherland route to lactaldehyde and thus to threonine aminonitrile (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Surface UV photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with Cu(I) cyanide and H2S reductant
conditions.other: 2 h irradiation; reported 19% yield of lactaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the cyanohydrin under H2S-driven Cu-cyanide cycling converts the nitrile-bearing alcohol precursor to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key experimentally demonstrated step of the cyanosulfidic threonine route, producing lactaldehyde in 19% yield under UV irradiation (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration in aqueous Strecker environment
conditions.other: anoxic; imine formation followed by cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine which is trapped by cyanide, yielding 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland described lactaldehyde as the direct aldehyde precursor to the threonine aminonitrile via classic Strecker chemistry; Patel et al. place this aminonitrile within cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized sugar pool with mineral concentration
conditions.other: parallel entry point to threonine aminonitrile

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes ammonia addition and cyanide capture to form the same threonine aminonitrile branch point.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. specifically identify a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde reacts with NH3 and HCN to produce the threonine aminonitrile, linking RNA precursor chemistry to amino acid precursor chemistry (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: prebiotic pool; yields racemic threonine and allothreonine mixture

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the nitrile to the amino acid carboxylate, furnishing threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: The final hydrolysis step from 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile to threonine is part of the classic Strecker route described for the lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde pathways; stereochemical output is mixed and racemic (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydroxyethyl hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration under ammonium carbonate-rich conditions
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type setting; thermodynamic sink via hydantoin

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and CO2/ammonium carbonate equivalents to form a hydantoin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: For threonine, the literature proposes lactaldehyde + HCN + NH3 + CO2 leading to 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the Bucherer-Bergs intermediate; this route is inferred by analogy to experimentally demonstrated keto-acid systems and is therefore included as literature-guided but not directly demonstrated for threonine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of hydroxyethyl hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: hydrolytic opening of hydantoin ring

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise hydantoin hydrolysis releases the alpha-amino acid skeleton as threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the terminating step of Bucherer-Bergs amino acid synthesis; hydantoins are also meteoritically relevant, though threonine-specific experimental closure remains inferential rather than directly shown (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner and Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge atmospheric synthesis of HCN, ammonia, and aldehydes including acetaldehyde

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge, ambient bulk aqueous trap
conditions.other: CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3 or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S gas mixtures; products collected in water

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates reactive radicals leading to HCN, NH3, and aldehydes that accumulate in aqueous solution for downstream Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Spark discharge experiments provided direct experimental detection of threonine and allothreonine from simple gas mixtures, implying in situ formation of HCN, NH3, and aldehydes such as lactaldehyde/acetaldehyde that feed Strecker pathways. This node explicitly decomposes the atmospheric source into mechanistic precursors as requested by prior feedback (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge aqueous formation of threonine from atmospheric precursor mixtures

conditions.other: racemic threonine/allothreonine; especially promoted in H2S-containing configurations

reactions.mechanism: Complex discharge chemistry generates aldehydes and cyanide precursors that undergo aqueous Strecker-type synthesis to yield threonine within a broader amino acid mixture.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction summarizes the directly observed outcome of Miller-Urey-type discharge experiments where threonine is detected among amino acid products; the exact route likely proceeds through in situ aldehydes and Strecker chemistry, but the observation itself is empirical (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Surface HCN oligomerization to heterogeneous nitrile precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation and concentration on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: HCN polymerization in concentrated cyanide settings

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes into complex nitrile-rich polymers and heterocyclic precursor mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: HCN polymerization is a broad, well-documented source of prebiotic organic complexity. Ruiz-Bermejo et al. review conditions where hydrolysis of HCN polymers yields multiple amino acids including threonine, though nonselectively. It is retained here as a weak but connected environmental source, not as a target-optimized route (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of HCN oligomer products to threonine-containing amino acid mixtures

conditions.other: highly nonselective hydrolysate; low threonine abundance relative to simpler amino acids

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic breakdown of heterogeneous HCN oligomers releases amino acids, including threonine among complex mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Threonine has been reported in hydrolysates of HCN polymers under multiple experimental regimes, but the route is unresolved and nonselective. It is therefore included only as a background source pathway and explicitly not treated as a strong dedicated threonine synthesis route (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Biochemical aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: thermodynamically unfavorable; requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; gives threonine and allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol relationship is reversed so glycine and acetaldehyde condense to form the beta-hydroxy alpha-amino acid skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: This route is conceptually motivated by the reverse of biological threonine aldolase cleavage. Literature notes the structural validity but also emphasizes lack of a purely prebiotic demonstration and unfavorable thermodynamics, so this branch is explicitly speculative but literature-grounded (Fesko, 2016).

reactions.name: Biochemical ammonia-aldehyde network producing glycine from formaldehyde-derived carbonyl pools

conditions.other: mixture chemistry, low selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia reacts within mixed aldehyde pools to furnish simple amino acids including glycine-related products.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka showed that aldehydes with ammonia in water give families of hydroxy amino acids and related amino acid products. This step is used conservatively only to source glycine for the aldol branch, not to claim selective threonine synthesis (Koga and Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Biochemical formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from pyruvate and formaldehyde-equivalent C1 chemistry

conditions.other: network-connecting step; chemically plausible homologation but not directly demonstrated for threonine precursor under prebiotic conditions

reactions.mechanism: C1 homologation and oxidation-state adjustment convert a keto-acid hub into the beta-hydroxy keto-acid precursor of threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as the keto precursor to threonine, but direct prebiotic synthesis of this specific intermediate is an open gap. This step is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because the deep research report names the precursor as central to any keto-acid route (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Biochemical reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: pH 8 for Ni/H2 conditions; principle established but threonine precursor not directly tested

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by reduction to the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Fe2+-promoted and Ni/H2-driven reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is experimentally established, but the specific precursor 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate was not tested. This route is retained as a principle-based, explicitly speculative threonine branch and kept in mild biochemical conditions to avoid the thermal-instability problem highlighted in the literature (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Biochemical wet-dry peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling to rapidly sequester unstable threonine into peptide products

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation forms a peptide bond, reducing free-threonine residence time in aqueous solution.
reactions.reasoning: Threonine is unusually unstable in prolonged aqueous settings, so rapid utilization is chemically important. Wet-dry peptide formation on mineral surfaces is a documented prebiotic strategy for amino acid capture and is included here as a downstream sink rather than a threonine-forming step (Lahav et al., 1978; Kitadai et al., 2015; Damer and Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which is converted through cyanohydrin reduction to lactaldehyde and then to threonine aminonitrile before hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde Strecker route from cyanosulfidic aldehydes
pathways.description: HCN-derived formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde homologate to glyceraldehyde, which directly forms the threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies acetaldehyde, which is transferred to surface cyanosulfidic chemistry for conversion to lactaldehyde, aminonitrile, and threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Reduced hydrothermal carbon feeds surface HCN photoredox aldehyde production and sugar homologation to glyceraldehyde, then Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde enters a Bucherer-Bergs sequence via hydroxyethyl hydantoin, which then hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-assisted Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation supplies acetaldehyde, and surface Bucherer-Bergs chemistry converts the derived lactaldehyde to hydantoin and threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct threonine route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge acting on simple gases directly yields threonine in aqueous product mixtures.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge decomposed Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge first generates HCN, ammonia, and acetaldehyde, which then feed the canonical cyanohydrin-lactaldehyde Strecker route to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate route
pathways.description: HCN oligomers formed in concentrated cyanide settings hydrolyze to complex amino acid mixtures containing threonine.

pathways.name: Alpha-keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is converted through a speculative but literature-guided beta-hydroxy keto-acid precursor, then reduced with ammonia and hydrogen to threonine under mild conditions.
